description: Year-long weekly weather almanac — Farmer's Almanac style for Midwest/Ohio

weeks.forecast: Winter's still hanging on, but you can feel the change coming. A few warm days will tease you, then Old Man Winter sneaks back in for one more round.
weeks.conditions: Mix of clouds and sun, chance of late snow flurries
weeks.gardening_tip: Start your tomato and pepper seeds indoors near a sunny window. They need about 6-8 weeks before transplanting.
weeks.folk_wisdom: If March comes in like a lion, it goes out like a lamb.

weeks.forecast: Temperatures start to swing — could be 55 one day and 30 the next. That's March for you. Keep layers handy and watch for mud season.
weeks.conditions: Variable — sunshine followed by gray skies and possible rain
weeks.gardening_tip: Prune your fruit trees and roses before the buds break. A clean cut now means healthier growth later.
weeks.folk_wisdom: A dry March and a wet May fill the barns with grain and hay.
weeks.sunrise_trend: Around 6:50 AM — daylight saving time ahead

weeks.forecast: Spring forward! Daylight saving brings longer evenings. Expect a mix of rain and mild temps — perfect weather for the first robins to show up.
weeks.conditions: Increasing rain showers, mild afternoons
weeks.gardening_tip: Time to work compost into your garden beds. The soil is thawing and ready to be fed.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When the robins come, spring is not far behind.

weeks.forecast: The daffodils are pushing through and the grass is starting to green up. Still cool mornings but afternoons are feeling more like spring.
weeks.conditions: Partly sunny with scattered showers
weeks.gardening_tip: Plant cold-hardy crops like peas, lettuce, and spinach directly in the garden. They can handle a light frost.
weeks.folk_wisdom: One swallow does not make a spring.

weeks.forecast: March going out gently this year. Expect pleasant days in the mid-50s with a few sprinkles. The forsythia should be blooming bright yellow.
weeks.conditions: Mostly pleasant with occasional light rain
weeks.gardening_tip: When the forsythia blooms, it's time to apply crabgrass preventer to the lawn.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Plant your peas on St. Patrick's Day and they'll be ready by the Fourth of July.

weeks.forecast: April showers are here right on schedule. Warm rains will green everything up fast. Don't put away the umbrella just yet.
weeks.conditions: Frequent rain showers, some thunderstorms possible
weeks.gardening_tip: Direct-sow carrots, beets, and radishes. They like the cool spring soil and steady rain.
weeks.folk_wisdom: April showers bring May flowers.

weeks.forecast: A beautiful stretch of spring weather is on the way. Dogwoods and redbuds are showing off. Enjoy it — this is Ohio at its finest.
weeks.conditions: Partly sunny with warm afternoons
weeks.gardening_tip: Set out your transplants of broccoli, cabbage, and cauliflower. They love this cool-warm balance.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When the dogwood blossoms, no more frost will come.

weeks.forecast: Mid-spring warmth is settling in. Lawns need their first mowing and the trees are leafing out. Watch for one more chilly night — frost isn't done yet.
weeks.conditions: Warm days, cool nights, chance of a stray frost
weeks.gardening_tip: Don't rush the tomatoes outside yet! Wait until after Mother's Day to be safe from frost.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Ne'er cast a clout till May be out.

weeks.forecast: Late April bringing warm days and cool nights. Perfect sleeping weather with the windows cracked open. Rain is tapering off some.
weeks.conditions: Pleasant with decreasing rain chances
weeks.gardening_tip: Mulch your garden beds now to hold in moisture and keep weeds down all summer long.
weeks.folk_wisdom: A late Easter means a late spring.

weeks.forecast: May is busting out all over. Warm and green everywhere you look. Still a slim chance of frost early in the month, so keep an eye on the overnight lows.
weeks.conditions: Warm and increasingly sunny
weeks.gardening_tip: Plant your annual flowers — marigolds, petunias, and zinnias brighten up any porch or garden bed.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Wet May brings plenty of hay.

weeks.forecast: Mother's Day weekend — traditionally the last frost date for Ohio. After this week, it's safe to plant just about anything. Warm and lovely.
weeks.conditions: Mostly sunny and warm, scattered afternoon showers
weeks.gardening_tip: Now's the time! Get those tomatoes, peppers, squash, and cucumbers in the ground. The soil is warm enough.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Plant your garden after Mother's Day and you'll rarely lose a thing to frost.

weeks.forecast: Warm days settling in for good now. Expect a few afternoon thunderstorms — the kind that roll through fast and leave everything smelling fresh.
weeks.conditions: Warm with afternoon thunderstorm chances
weeks.gardening_tip: Keep up with weeding while they're small. A few minutes each morning saves hours later.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Thunder in spring, cold will bring.

weeks.forecast: Memorial Day weekend kicks off summer. Warm and humid with temperatures pushing into the upper 70s. Perfect for cookouts and planting the last of the garden.
weeks.conditions: Warm and humid, isolated storms
weeks.gardening_tip: Plant your green beans now — they grow fast in warm soil and you'll be picking in 6 weeks.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Corn should be knee-high by the Fourth of July.

weeks.forecast: Summer is here in everything but name. Long warm days and firefly evenings. The humidity is creeping up but it's not unbearable yet.
weeks.conditions: Mostly sunny and warm, building humidity
weeks.gardening_tip: Start harvesting lettuce and spinach before the heat makes them bolt and turn bitter.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When the June bugs swarm, warm days are the norm.

weeks.forecast: Warm and sunny with those classic Ohio summer afternoons. Pop-up thunderstorms in the late afternoon — you can almost set your watch by them.
weeks.conditions: Hot afternoons, pop-up thunderstorms
weeks.gardening_tip: Water your garden in the early morning, not at night. It helps prevent fungus and disease on the leaves.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Red sky at night, sailor's delight. Red sky in morning, sailor's warning.
weeks.sunrise_trend: Around 6:08 AM — near the earliest sunrise

weeks.forecast: Father's Day week — expect hot and sunny. The kind of week where you want to sit on the porch with a cold glass of lemonade.
weeks.conditions: Hot and sunny, slight chance of evening storms
weeks.gardening_tip: Side-dress your corn and tomatoes with a little fertilizer. They're hungry growers this time of year.
weeks.folk_wisdom: If it rains on June 15th, it means a wet harvest.
weeks.sunrise_trend: Around 6:08 AM — earliest sunrise of the year

weeks.forecast: Summer solstice — the longest day of the year! Enjoy every minute of daylight. Hot and steamy with the first real heat wave possible.
weeks.conditions: Hot and humid, heat advisory possible
weeks.gardening_tip: Mulch heavily around your tomatoes to keep the soil cool and moist. They'll thank you with bigger fruit.
weeks.folk_wisdom: The longest day brings the shortest night, and from here the sun begins its flight.

weeks.forecast: Hot and sticky as we head into July. Keep hydrated and take it easy during the afternoon heat. Evening walks are the way to go.
weeks.conditions: Hot and humid with scattered thunderstorms
weeks.gardening_tip: Pick your zucchini and summer squash when they're small — they taste better and the plant keeps producing.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When the dew is on the grass, rain will never come to pass.

weeks.forecast: The dog days are setting in. Hot, humid, and hazy. Afternoon storms can be a blessing — they cool things off for a bit. Stay cool out there.
weeks.conditions: Hot and hazy, afternoon thunderstorms likely
weeks.gardening_tip: Keep picking those beans, cucumbers, and tomatoes. The more you pick, the more they produce.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Dog days bright and clear indicate a happy year.

weeks.forecast: Peak summer heat. The kind of week where the pavement shimmers and the cicadas are singing loud. Find some shade and keep a fan going.
weeks.conditions: Very hot and humid, strong storms possible
weeks.gardening_tip: If your tomato leaves are curling, don't panic — that's just their way of coping with the heat. Keep watering.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When the cicadas sing, the heat of summer they bring.

weeks.forecast: Mid to late July — still hot but we might catch a break with a cool front. Enjoy those cooler mornings when they come; they're a preview of what's ahead.
weeks.conditions: Hot with a possible cool front mid-week
weeks.gardening_tip: Plant a fall crop of green beans now. They'll be ready to pick in September when the weather cools.
weeks.folk_wisdom: If July is very hot, the winter will be cold.

weeks.forecast: Late July warmth holding steady. The corn is tasseling and the gardens are in full swing. Evenings are starting to feel just a touch shorter.
weeks.conditions: Warm and muggy, scattered storms
weeks.gardening_tip: Start thinking about your fall garden. Order seeds for kale, turnips, and late-season lettuce.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When corn tassels, summer has reached its peak.

weeks.forecast: August heat is on. Everything in the garden is ripening at once — tomatoes, peppers, corn. It's canning season! Stay cool and pace yourself.
weeks.conditions: Hot and humid, typical summer pattern
weeks.gardening_tip: Time to start canning and preserving. Tomato sauce, salsa, pickles — put up summer's bounty for winter.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Dry August and warm does harvest no harm.

weeks.forecast: The heat holds but the days are noticeably shorter. You can feel the season starting to turn, even though summer's not ready to let go just yet.
weeks.conditions: Warm days, pleasant evenings emerging
weeks.gardening_tip: Plant broccoli and cauliflower transplants now for a fall harvest. They do better in cooler weather anyway.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When the katydids begin to sing, six weeks until the first frost they bring.

weeks.forecast: Late August — the mornings have that first hint of fall crispness. Afternoons are still warm but the edge is off. County fair weather!
weeks.conditions: Warm but less humid, pleasant evenings
weeks.gardening_tip: Sow spinach and lettuce for a fall salad garden. The cooler temps coming will be just what they need.
weeks.folk_wisdom: If the first week of August is unusually warm, the coming winter will be snowy and long.

weeks.forecast: Summer's last hurrah. Kids heading back to school and the evenings are drawing in. Enjoy these warm days — they'll be memories before you know it.
weeks.conditions: Pleasant and warm with lower humidity
weeks.gardening_tip: Keep watering those fall transplants. They need consistent moisture to get established before cooler weather.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When you can hear the train from far away, rain is on the way.

weeks.forecast: September sneaks in with a gorgeous week. Cool mornings, warm afternoons, and that golden light that makes everything look like a painting.
weeks.conditions: Beautiful early fall weather, mostly sunny
weeks.gardening_tip: Plant garlic cloves now — they'll overwinter and give you big beautiful bulbs next summer.
weeks.folk_wisdom: September dries up ditches or breaks down bridges.

weeks.forecast: Labor Day weekend wraps up the unofficial summer. Expect comfortable temps and clear skies. Perfect for one last picnic or a drive to see the first hints of color.
weeks.conditions: Clear and comfortable, ideal early fall weather
weeks.gardening_tip: Start pulling out spent summer plants and composting them. Your garden soil will thank you next spring.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Fair on September first, fair for the month.

weeks.forecast: The air has that autumn crispness now. Leaves are just starting to turn at the tips. Apple cider season is here — enjoy every sip.
weeks.conditions: Cool mornings, warm sunny afternoons
weeks.gardening_tip: Harvest your winter squash and pumpkins when the stems start to dry. Cure them in the sun for a few days.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When squirrels gather nuts in a flurry, winter will come in a hurry.

weeks.forecast: Autumn equinox — day and night are equal. The maples are blushing red and orange. Cool nights make for perfect sleeping weather.
weeks.conditions: Cool and clear with increasing color in the trees
weeks.gardening_tip: Overseed your lawn now. Fall is the best time — the seeds love the warm soil and cool air.
weeks.folk_wisdom: As the days shorten, so do the tempers of the weather.

weeks.forecast: Peak fall color is building. Gorgeous drives through the countryside this week. A light frost is possible in the low spots — cover those tender plants.
weeks.conditions: Cool and colorful, frost possible in valleys
weeks.gardening_tip: Bring in your houseplants that summered outside before the first frost gets them.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When leaves fall early, autumn and winter will be mild.

weeks.forecast: October glory! The trees are on fire with color and the air smells like wood smoke and apples. Chilly mornings and warm flannel afternoons.
weeks.conditions: Cool and crisp, peak fall foliage
weeks.gardening_tip: Plant spring-blooming bulbs — tulips, daffodils, and crocuses go in the ground now for a beautiful surprise in April.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Much rain in October, much wind in December.

weeks.forecast: Sweater weather is here to stay. Expect some rainy days mixed with those perfect blue-sky autumn afternoons. The leaves are starting to fall.
weeks.conditions: Mix of sun and rain, leaves falling
weeks.gardening_tip: Rake those leaves and compost them or use them as mulch. They're garden gold.
weeks.folk_wisdom: If October brings heavy frost and wind, then January and February will be mild.

weeks.forecast: The trees are going bare and the sky seems bigger. Chilly rain showers move through. Time to check the furnace and make sure it's ready.
weeks.conditions: Cool and breezy with passing showers
weeks.gardening_tip: Clean and oil your garden tools before storing them for winter. A little care now means they'll last for years.
weeks.folk_wisdom: A warm October means a cold February.

weeks.forecast: Halloween week brings gray skies and that damp chill. The last of the leaves are coming down. First hard frost is likely this week if it hasn't come already.
weeks.conditions: Gray and chilly, hard frost likely
weeks.gardening_tip: Pull up your tomato cages and bean poles. Store them in the shed so they don't rust over winter.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When the woolly bear caterpillar's brown band is wide, a mild winter is on the way.

weeks.forecast: November arrives with bare trees and steel-gray skies. Fall back on the clocks — enjoy the extra hour. Early sunsets and cozy evenings ahead.
weeks.conditions: Overcast and cool, chance of rain
weeks.gardening_tip: Apply a thick layer of mulch over your perennial beds to protect the roots through winter.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Ice in November to bear a duck, the rest of winter will be rain and muck.

weeks.forecast: Raw and chilly. The kind of week where a warm bowl of soup hits the spot. Possible flurries toward the weekend — nothing that'll stick yet.
weeks.conditions: Cloudy and raw, first flurries possible
weeks.gardening_tip: Drain and store your garden hoses before they freeze and crack. Shut off outdoor faucets too.
weeks.folk_wisdom: If the first snow falls on unfrozen ground, expect a mild winter ahead.

weeks.forecast: Getting colder now. Frost every morning and the ground is starting to harden. But there's beauty in the bare branches against the sunset.
weeks.conditions: Frosty mornings, cool and breezy afternoons
weeks.gardening_tip: Now's a great time to test your garden soil. Send a sample to the extension office — you'll get results back before spring planting.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Wind from the south has rain in its mouth.

weeks.forecast: Thanksgiving week. Could go either way — maybe a mild spell for the holiday or maybe the first real snow. Either way, it's a week for gratitude and warm kitchens.
weeks.conditions: Variable — could be mild or snowy
weeks.gardening_tip: After Thanksgiving dinner, save those turkey bones and vegetable scraps for compost or garden-bed soup stock.
weeks.folk_wisdom: A green Thanksgiving means a white Christmas.

weeks.forecast: Winter is settling in. Short days and long nights. Bundle up for any outdoor time and keep the bird feeders full — the cardinals and blue jays will brighten your view.
weeks.conditions: Cold with mix of clouds and occasional snow
weeks.gardening_tip: Fill your bird feeders with sunflower seeds and suet. The birds will repay you by eating garden pests come spring.
weeks.folk_wisdom: As the days begin to lengthen, the cold begins to strengthen.

weeks.forecast: Cold and gray with that midwinter chill. Snow showers are likely. The kind of week for hot chocolate, good books, and Christmas decorating.
weeks.conditions: Cold with snow showers, accumulation possible
weeks.gardening_tip: Enjoy the rest! Winter is your garden's off-season. Browse seed catalogs and dream about next year's garden.
weeks.folk_wisdom: A heavy December snow keeps the ground warm for spring seeds below.

weeks.forecast: Deep winter cold is here. Highs barely touching freezing some days. Make sure the pipes are protected and the driveway salt is handy.
weeks.conditions: Cold and snowy, icy patches possible
weeks.gardening_tip: Check on any root vegetables still in the ground under mulch — carrots and parsnips actually get sweeter after a frost.
weeks.folk_wisdom: Clear moon, frost soon.

weeks.forecast: Christmas week and the winter solstice — the shortest day of the year. From here, the light slowly returns. Whether it's a white Christmas or not, it's a beautiful time.
weeks.conditions: Cold, chance of a white Christmas
weeks.gardening_tip: Give the gift of seeds, garden tools, or a nice houseplant. Gardeners always appreciate something that grows.
weeks.folk_wisdom: If Christmas Day be bright and clear, there'll be two winters in the year.

weeks.forecast: Year's end. Cold and quiet. A good week for reflection and rest. Watch for ice on the roads and keep the woodpile stocked if you've got a fireplace.
weeks.conditions: Cold and quiet, possible freezing rain
weeks.gardening_tip: Start planning next year's garden on paper. Rotate where you plant things to keep the soil healthy.
weeks.folk_wisdom: If New Year's Eve wind blows south, it betokens warmth and growth.

weeks.forecast: Welcome to the new year. January in Ohio means business — cold, snowy, and not much daylight. But the days ARE getting longer, one minute at a time.
weeks.conditions: Cold and snowy, below-average temps possible
weeks.gardening_tip: Order your seed catalogs if you haven't already. Burpee, Baker Creek, and Johnny's are all good ones.
weeks.folk_wisdom: If January is mild, the rest of winter will be wild.

weeks.forecast: This is often the coldest week of the year in Ohio. Single digits overnight are possible. Stay inside where it's warm and let the furnace earn its keep.
weeks.conditions: Bitterly cold, wind chill advisory possible
weeks.gardening_tip: Check on your stored potatoes, onions, and squash. Remove any that are going soft before they spoil the others.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When the cat lies on its brain, it's going to rain. When it sits with its back to the fire, cold weather is near.

weeks.forecast: Still deep in winter but the light is noticeably returning. Sunsets are later and that counts for something. Expect snow and cold but spring is only 8 weeks away.
weeks.conditions: Cold with periodic snow, some sunny breaks
weeks.gardening_tip: Start onion seeds indoors now. They need a long growing season and benefit from an early start.
weeks.folk_wisdom: A January fog will freeze a hog.

weeks.forecast: Late January — we're past the worst of it. Still cold, but there's a stubborn hopefulness in the air. Watch for a January thaw — a few days of 40s that tease you.
weeks.conditions: Cold with possible January thaw mid-week
weeks.gardening_tip: Clean and sharpen your pruning shears. You'll need them in about 6 weeks when it's time to prune.
weeks.folk_wisdom: The January thaw is nature's way of reminding us that spring always comes.

weeks.forecast: February starts cold but there's a shift in the air. The sun feels warmer on your face even when the thermometer says otherwise. Groundhog Day is coming!
weeks.conditions: Cold but gradually moderating, snow showers
weeks.gardening_tip: Start your herb seeds indoors — basil, parsley, and chives. Put them under a grow light or your sunniest window.
weeks.folk_wisdom: If Candlemas Day be fair and bright, winter will have another flight. If it be dark with clouds and rain, winter is gone and won't come again.

weeks.forecast: Valentine's week. Cold outside but warm hearts inside. We might see a mix of snow and rain — that slushy February weather that means winter is loosening its grip.
weeks.conditions: Mix of snow and rain, slush on the roads
weeks.gardening_tip: Start your pepper and eggplant seeds indoors. They need 8-10 weeks before transplanting.
weeks.folk_wisdom: February sunshine is not always what it seems; it lights but does not warm.

weeks.forecast: The back of winter is breaking. Longer days, occasional warm spells in the 40s and 50s. The sap is starting to run in the maple trees — sugaring season!
weeks.conditions: Variable — cold snaps mixed with mild days
weeks.gardening_tip: Prune your grape vines and berry bushes while they're still dormant. The sap hasn't risen yet.
weeks.folk_wisdom: When maple sap runs, spring is not far behind.

weeks.forecast: The final week of winter. The crocuses might be brave enough to poke through the snow. March is right around the corner and with it, a new beginning.
weeks.conditions: Moderating temperatures, early crocuses possible
weeks.gardening_tip: Start your tomato seeds indoors now! Six to eight weeks before the last frost is perfect timing.
weeks.folk_wisdom: No matter how long the winter, spring is sure to follow.
